prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I have everything I need. The diagnosis is crystal clear. Here is the full breakdown:

---

## Diagnosis

Your content edits are **permanently stuck in the route file and will never reach the live site on their own.** The file `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is not a component that renders content — it is a **one-shot CMS seeding script** disguised as a Next.js route handler. Its sole job is: when `POST`-ed to, it calls Payload's local API to upsert the essay document in the database. The live site reads from that database at request time. It does not read from the route file at all.

The chain looks like this:

```
route.ts  ──POST──▶  Payload DB  ──query──▶  Live page
   ↑
 (you edited this,
  but never fired
  the POST)
```

You edited the source of truth for the seed, but never pulled the trigger. The database still holds the old content, so the live page renders the old content. The file save was successful — the route file is correct. The database was simply never told.

---

## Root Cause

**EAP-087 — Modifying a Content Seeding Route Without Calling the Endpoint**
(enforced by Guardrail `guardrail-engineering-25`)

> *"Editing the route file is not the same as running it. The file defines what will be written to the database; the HTTP call is what writes it. Saving the file produces no side effect on the database."*

This is the exact pattern logged in **ENG-149**, which documents a prior identical incident on this same file. It has now recurred.

---

## Fix

### Immediate — push the new content into the database

With your dev server running (port 3000 or whichever is active), fire the endpoint:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro
```

Or from a browser tab, open the dev-tools console and run:

```js
fetch('/api/update-etro', { method: 'POST' })
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(console.log)
```

You should get a `200` response with a success payload. After that, hard-refresh the ETRO essay page — the new content will be live immediately in dev. Deploy as normal and it will be live in production after the next Vercel build, because production also reads from the database (not the file).

### Verify it actually landed

After the POST, check one distinguishing string from your new content exists in the rendered page — e.g. the opening line of the new blurb — before committing or deploying.

---

## Correct Workflow for All Future Content Work

Every time you touch any `update-*/route.ts` seeding file, the session is not done until the endpoint has been called and the rendered page confirms the change. The canonical three-step sequence is:

| Step | Action | Why |
|------|--------|-----|
| 1 | Edit the route file with new content | Prepares the seed |
| 2 | `curl -X POST http://localhost:<port>/api/update-<slug>` | Writes to the Payload DB |
| 3 | Verify the rendered page shows the new content | Confirms the DB write succeeded |

Only after Step 3 do you commit and ship. The commit should include the route file change (so the seed is reproducible), but the page content is live the moment Step 2 completes — not the moment Step 1 does.

A useful mental model: **treat these route files exactly like database migration scripts.** Writing a migration file does not run the migration. You always have to execute it separately.

---

## Principle

The root issue is a **broken mental model of where the content actually lives.** In a Payload CMS project, the source of truth for what users see is the **database**, not the source files. Route files in `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-*/` are *imperatives*, not *declarations* — they describe a write operation that only happens if you invoke them. Editing them without invoking them is like editing a shell script and wondering why nothing changed on the server. Guardrail `guardrail-engineering-25` exists precisely because this mistake is easy to make and invisible until you look at the live page.
